title: The Smell of Paper: What Is Gained and What Is Lost When Archives Go Digital
summary: A senior and junior archivist discuss the implications of digitising historical records — from the sensory and material knowledge that survives only in physical documents to the hidden assumptions baked into digitisation decisions.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: I want to be clear that I am not a Luddite about this. The digitisation programme has made our collection accessible to researchers in Vietnam, in Argentina, in rural Scotland — people who could never have come here in person. That is an unambiguous good, and I would feel dishonest if I didn't acknowledge it first.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: I have reservations that I find myself unable to adequately articulate, which is perhaps the most disturbing aspect of the problem. When you work with a document over many years, you develop a relationship with it that is partly sensory. The foxing on a page tells you something about the storage conditions it has survived. The quality of the ink tells you about the materials available to the writer. The pressure variations in a handwritten letter tell you about the emotional state of the person who wrote it. None of that is captured in a digital scan.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: I take your point about the sensory dimension, but isn't that a problem of current technology rather than digitisation in principle? We already have multispectral imaging that reveals text invisible to the naked eye. Three-dimensional scanning can capture paper texture and seal impressions. The technology is converging on the physical object in ways that were unimaginable twenty years ago.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: Multispectral imaging captures what we already know to look for. The problem with digital surrogates is that they are, by definition, the product of decisions made in advance: decisions about resolution, about colour calibration, about what counts as a meaningful feature worth capturing. The physical document does not make those decisions. It simply is. And a researcher fifty years from now, asking a question we cannot currently anticipate, may need to examine an aspect of the original that our digitisation protocols never thought to record.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: That argument could justify never digitising anything. Every technology involves decisions about what to capture and what to discard. Photography made similar choices. A written transcript makes choices. Even a catalogue entry embeds assumptions about what is significant.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: I grant that entirely. The difference is that when you photograph an object or write a catalogue entry, the original remains. The original can be consulted again. The crisis — and I think it is a crisis, though a slow-moving one — comes when digitisation is accompanied by deaccessioning: when the logic of 'we have a digital copy, we no longer need the physical object' begins to take hold.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: In some institutions, yes. Not dramatically, not often announced, but through a gradual shift in acquisition policy, in storage decisions, in what gets transferred to off-site facilities that in practice means items are never consulted again. The physical record retreats from practical accessibility one administrative decision at a time.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: I want to challenge something else you said earlier — about the authority of the original. There is an assumption in archival practice that the physical document is the authoritative object, and that the digital surrogate is secondary. But that assumes we can trust our assessment of what constitutes the original. Many of the documents in our collection are themselves copies — scribal copies, official transcriptions, copies made when the prior version was deteriorating. At what point does a copy become the record?

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: That is a genuine and important complication. And it applies with particular force to documents that have already been through multiple mediating processes: translated, transcribed, printed, microfilmed, and now scanned. At each stage, something is interpreted and something is lost. I would not deny that. But the accumulation of mediating layers does not reduce the value of what remains — it makes it more important to preserve what remains, not less.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: I think what I find most troubling about the current moment is less the technology than the institutional context in which it is being deployed. Digitisation is expensive, and it is being funded, in many cases, by partnerships with commercial entities whose interests are not identical to those of public access and long-term preservation. When a technology company funds the digitisation of a national archive, they acquire data about how researchers interact with documents, they may hold licences that complicate future re-digitisation, and they shape the metadata standards that determine how documents are findable.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: Now that is something I have been trying to put into words for years. The authority of the original has always been contested, but it was contested on scholarly grounds, within the community of those who cared about documents. What is new is that the infrastructure of access is being built by actors whose relationship to scholarship is instrumental at best.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: And who are systematically removing from the archive the traces of their own involvement. The digitisation project becomes invisible — it appears as neutral provision of access, when it is in fact a deeply consequential series of interpretive decisions made by people with commercial incentives.

body.speaker: Edmund
body.text: Which is why I believe the profession needs to insist that digitisation is never merely technical. Every scan is an editorial act. Every metadata schema is an argument about what matters. And the physical object — whatever its condition, whatever its status as copy or original — is the one thing that resists being entirely subsumed into someone else's framework.

body.speaker: Vivienne
body.text: I find myself agreeing more than I expected to. Perhaps what the digital transformation of the archive demands is not a defence of the physical against the digital, but a much more assertive archival politics — a claim that these decisions belong to the profession, and to the public the profession serves, not to whoever happens to hold the digitisation contract.

annotations.meaningVi: người chống đối công nghệ mới — xuất phát từ phong trào Luddite thế kỷ 19 phản đối cơ giới hóa
annotations.exampleEn: He was no Luddite — he embraced technology when it served a clear purpose.

annotations.meaningVi: đốm gỉ sét trên giấy cũ — vết ố màu nâu đỏ xuất hiện trên giấy cũ do độ ẩm và nấm mốc
annotations.exampleEn: The foxing on the pages indicated the manuscript had been stored in damp conditions.

annotations.meaningVi: chụp ảnh đa phổ — kỹ thuật sử dụng nhiều bước sóng ánh sáng để tiết lộ thông tin ẩn trong tài liệu
annotations.exampleEn: Multispectral imaging recovered erased text from the damaged palimpsest.

annotations.meaningVi: bản thay thế kỹ thuật số — bản sao kỹ thuật số thay thế cho vật gốc trong việc tra cứu
annotations.exampleEn: The digital surrogate allowed remote researchers to study the manuscript without handling the fragile original.

annotations.meaningVi: cân chỉnh màu sắc — quá trình đảm bảo màu sắc trong bản số hóa trùng khớp với màu sắc thực của vật gốc
annotations.exampleEn: Inconsistent colour calibration across different digitisation projects makes comparison difficult.

annotations.meaningVi: quy trình số hóa — bộ tiêu chuẩn và thủ tục xác định cách số hóa tài liệu
annotations.exampleEn: The institution updated its digitisation protocols to include capture of watermarks and paper texture.

annotations.meaningVi: loại bỏ khỏi bộ sưu tập — quy trình chính thức xóa bỏ một vật phẩm khỏi danh mục lưu trữ và có thể xử lý nó
annotations.exampleEn: The museum's deaccessioning of its ethnographic collection provoked protests from source communities.

annotations.meaningVi: chính sách thu nhận — các tiêu chí quyết định những gì được đưa vào bộ sưu tập lưu trữ
annotations.exampleEn: A shift in acquisition policy towards digital-born records has transformed the nature of the archive.

annotations.meaningVi: bản chép tay — bản sao được thực hiện bởi thư lại hoặc người sao chép thủ công
annotations.exampleEn: Many classical texts survive only as scribal copies made centuries after the original was lost.

annotations.meaningVi: các quy trình trung gian — các bước xử lý, sao chép hoặc chuyển đổi can thiệp giữa tài liệu gốc và hình thức hiện tại
annotations.exampleEn: Each mediating process — translation, printing, microfilming — introduces potential distortion.

annotations.meaningVi: tiêu chuẩn siêu dữ liệu — các quy tắc mô tả thông tin về tài liệu để giúp tìm kiếm và tổ chức
annotations.exampleEn: Without agreed metadata standards, digitised collections from different institutions cannot interoperate.

annotations.meaningVi: mang tính công cụ — coi thứ gì đó chỉ có giá trị vì lợi ích nó mang lại, không phải vì giá trị nội tại
annotations.exampleEn: His interest in the project was purely instrumental — he wanted access to the data, not to contribute to the research.

annotations.meaningVi: hành động biên tập — quyết định về những gì được đưa vào hoặc loại bỏ trong quá trình tái tạo một tài liệu
annotations.exampleEn: Choosing which documents to scan first is an editorial act that privileges certain histories over others.

annotations.meaningVi: lược đồ siêu dữ liệu — cấu trúc tổ chức thông tin mô tả về tài liệu
annotations.exampleEn: The metadata schema embedded assumptions about authorship that disadvantaged collaborative and anonymous works.

annotations.meaningVi: chính trị lưu trữ — cuộc tranh luận và đấu tranh quyền lực xung quanh ai kiểm soát hồ sơ lịch sử
annotations.exampleEn: Post-colonial archival politics questions who has the right to hold and control colonial-era records.

annotations.meaningVi: bị bao gồm, bị hấp thụ vào một cái gì đó lớn hơn và mất đi sự độc lập
annotations.exampleEn: Local customs were gradually subsumed into a homogenised national culture.

annotations.meaningVi: biến thiên áp lực — sự khác biệt về lực ép của bút lên giấy, cho biết trạng thái tâm lý của người viết
annotations.exampleEn: Forensic handwriting analysis examines pressure variations to detect emotional states or attempted forgery.

annotations.meaningVi: dấu ấn — hình ảnh để lại trên sáp hoặc giấy bởi con dấu, thường xác nhận tính xác thực
annotations.exampleEn: The seal impressions on the treaty confirmed its authenticity and the identity of the signatories.

annotations.meaningVi: bản viết tay được viết đè — tài liệu viết tay mà văn bản gốc đã bị xóa hoặc cạo để viết lại, nhưng dấu vết cũ vẫn còn
annotations.exampleEn: The medieval palimpsest contained a classical text beneath later Christian writing.

annotations.meaningVi: đang tiến gần đến, tiếp cận dần với
annotations.exampleEn: Several research teams are independently converging on the same theoretical solution.

grammarNotes.title: Concession with 'grant' and 'I would not deny' — academic acknowledgment without capitulation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tranh luận học thuật, các cụm từ như 'I grant that entirely', 'I would not deny that', và 'I take your point' được dùng để thừa nhận một điểm của đối phương mà không từ bỏ lập trường chính. Edmund dùng 'I grant that entirely' khi Vivienne chỉ ra rằng mọi công nghệ ghi chép đều có hạn chế — nhưng ngay sau đó ông xác định điều gì làm cho số hóa kèm theo deaccessioning khác biệt. Đây là chiến lược quan trọng trong tiếng Anh học thuật: thừa nhận để xây dựng uy tín trước khi tiếp tục lập luận.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I grant that entirely' and 'I would not deny that' are concessive speech acts that acknowledge the opponent's point without abandoning the argument. They signal intellectual honesty and build credibility. Note the structure: concession + qualification ('The difference is…', 'But the accumulation of…'). The concession is always followed by a 'but' or 'the difference is' that re-establishes the main line. This pattern is a hallmark of mature academic argumentation in English.

grammarNotes.title: Nominalization as argument compression — 'The authority of the original', 'The crisis of deaccessioning'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Danh từ hóa biến các quá trình và hành động phức tạp thành danh từ, cho phép nói về chúng như những thực thể có thể lập luận, phủ nhận hoặc bảo vệ. 'The authority of the original' nén cả một cuộc tranh luận về tính xác thực thành một cụm danh từ có thể được 'thách thức', 'khẳng định', hoặc 'xem xét'. Tương tự, 'the infrastructure of access' biến một loạt quyết định kỹ thuật và chính sách phức tạp thành một thực thể duy nhất mà Vivienne có thể lập luận về ai kiểm soát nó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Nominalizations like 'the authority of the original', 'the infrastructure of access', and 'the accumulation of mediating layers' compress complex processes into noun phrases that can be debated as unified entities. 'The authority of the original has always been contested, but it was contested on scholarly grounds' — here two instances of nominalisation ('authority of the original' and 'scholarly grounds') allow Edmund to make a complex historical-political argument in a single sentence. This is characteristic of high-register academic discourse.

translationVi:
Edmund: Tôi muốn nói rõ rằng tôi không phải là người chống đối công nghệ. Chương trình số hóa đã làm cho bộ sưu tập của chúng ta tiếp cận được với các nhà nghiên cứu ở Việt Nam, ở Argentina, ở vùng nông thôn Scotland — những người không bao giờ có thể đến đây trực tiếp. Đó là điều tốt rõ ràng, và tôi sẽ cảm thấy không trung thực nếu không thừa nhận điều đó trước.
Vivienne: Và tuy nhiên ông vẫn có những băn khoăn.
Edmund: Tôi có những băn khoăn mà tôi thấy mình không thể diễn đạt đầy đủ, đó có lẽ là khía cạnh đáng lo ngại nhất của vấn đề. Khi bạn làm việc với một tài liệu trong nhiều năm, bạn phát triển một mối quan hệ với nó phần nào mang tính cảm quan. Những đốm gỉ sét trên trang giấy cho bạn biết về điều kiện lưu trữ mà nó đã sống sót qua. Chất lượng mực cho bạn biết về các vật liệu mà người viết có thể tiếp cận. Các biến thiên áp lực trong một bức thư viết tay cho bạn biết về trạng thái cảm xúc của người đã viết nó. Không có điều nào trong số đó được ghi lại trong một bản quét kỹ thuật số.
Vivienne: Tôi hiểu điểm ông nói về chiều cảm quan, nhưng liệu đó có phải là vấn đề của công nghệ hiện tại hơn là số hóa về mặt nguyên tắc không? Chúng ta đã có chụp ảnh đa phổ tiết lộ văn bản vô hình với mắt thường. Quét ba chiều có thể nắm bắt kết cấu giấy và dấu ấn. Công nghệ đang hội tụ về đối tượng vật lý theo những cách không thể tưởng tượng hai mươi năm trước.
Edmund: Chụp ảnh đa phổ nắm bắt những gì chúng ta đã biết cần tìm. Vấn đề với các bản thay thế kỹ thuật số là chúng, theo định nghĩa, là sản phẩm của các quyết định được đưa ra trước: quyết định về độ phân giải, về cân chỉnh màu sắc, về những gì được coi là một đặc điểm có ý nghĩa đáng nắm bắt. Tài liệu vật lý không đưa ra những quyết định đó. Nó đơn giản là nó. Và một nhà nghiên cứu năm mươi năm sau, đặt ra câu hỏi mà chúng ta hiện không thể dự liệu, có thể cần kiểm tra một khía cạnh của bản gốc mà các quy trình số hóa của chúng ta chưa bao giờ nghĩ đến việc ghi lại.
Vivienne: Lập luận đó có thể biện hộ cho việc không bao giờ số hóa bất cứ thứ gì. Mọi công nghệ đều liên quan đến quyết định về những gì cần nắm bắt và những gì cần bỏ đi. Nhiếp ảnh đã đưa ra những lựa chọn tương tự. Một bản ghi chép viết cũng đưa ra lựa chọn. Ngay cả một mục lục cũng nhúng vào những giả định về điều gì là quan trọng.
Edmund: Tôi hoàn toàn thừa nhận điều đó. Sự khác biệt là khi bạn chụp ảnh một đối tượng hoặc viết một mục lục, bản gốc vẫn còn. Bản gốc có thể được tham khảo lại. Cuộc khủng hoảng — và tôi nghĩ đó là một cuộc khủng hoảng, dù là một cuộc khủng hoảng chuyển động chậm — đến khi số hóa được kết hợp với loại bỏ khỏi bộ sưu tập: khi logic 'chúng ta đã có bản sao kỹ thuật số, chúng ta không còn cần đối tượng vật lý' bắt đầu được áp dụng.
Vivienne: Điều đó đang xảy ra?
Edmund: Ở một số cơ sở, có. Không ấn tượng, không thường được thông báo, nhưng thông qua sự thay đổi dần dần trong chính sách thu nhận, trong các quyết định lưu trữ, trong những gì được chuyển đến các cơ sở ngoài địa điểm mà trên thực tế có nghĩa là các mục không bao giờ được tham khảo lại. Hồ sơ vật lý rút lui khỏi khả năng tiếp cận thực tế một quyết định hành chính mỗi lần.
Vivienne: Tôi muốn thách thức điều khác ông nói trước đó — về quyền uy của bản gốc. Có một giả định trong thực hành lưu trữ rằng tài liệu vật lý là đối tượng có thẩm quyền, và bản thay thế kỹ thuật số là thứ cấp. Nhưng điều đó giả định chúng ta có thể tin vào đánh giá của mình về những gì tạo thành bản gốc. Nhiều tài liệu trong bộ sưu tập của chúng ta tự bản thân là bản sao — bản chép tay, bản ghi chép chính thức, bản sao được tạo ra khi phiên bản trước đó đang xấu đi. Tại thời điểm nào thì một bản sao trở thành hồ sơ?
Edmund: Đó là một sự phức tạp thực sự và quan trọng. Và nó áp dụng với sức mạnh đặc biệt cho các tài liệu đã trải qua nhiều quy trình trung gian: được dịch, được ghi chép, được in, được sao chụp vi phim, và bây giờ được quét. Ở mỗi giai đoạn, điều gì đó được giải thích và điều gì đó bị mất. Tôi sẽ không phủ nhận điều đó. Nhưng sự tích lũy của các lớp trung gian không làm giảm giá trị của những gì còn lại — nó làm cho việc bảo tồn những gì còn lại quan trọng hơn, không phải ít hơn.
Vivienne: Tôi nghĩ điều tôi thấy đáng lo ngại nhất về thời điểm hiện tại ít hơn là về công nghệ mà là về bối cảnh thể chế mà nó đang được triển khai. Số hóa thì tốn kém, và nó đang được tài trợ, trong nhiều trường hợp, bởi các quan hệ đối tác với các thực thể thương mại có lợi ích không đồng nhất với những lợi ích của việc tiếp cận công cộng và bảo tồn lâu dài. Khi một công ty công nghệ tài trợ cho việc số hóa một kho lưu trữ quốc gia, họ thu thập dữ liệu về cách các nhà nghiên cứu tương tác với tài liệu, họ có thể giữ các giấy phép làm phức tạp việc số hóa lại trong tương lai, và họ định hình các tiêu chuẩn siêu dữ liệu xác định cách tài liệu có thể được tìm thấy.
Edmund: Đó chính xác là điều tôi đã cố gắng diễn đạt trong nhiều năm. Quyền uy của bản gốc luôn bị tranh cãi, nhưng nó bị tranh cãi trên cơ sở học thuật, trong cộng đồng những người quan tâm đến tài liệu. Điều mới là cơ sở hạ tầng của việc tiếp cận đang được xây dựng bởi các tác nhân có mối quan hệ với học thuật là công cụ trong trường hợp tốt nhất.
Vivienne: Và những người đang có hệ thống xóa bỏ dấu vết can thiệp của chính họ khỏi kho lưu trữ. Dự án số hóa trở nên vô hình — nó xuất hiện như là sự cung cấp trung lập của quyền truy cập, trong khi thực tế là một loạt quyết định diễn giải sâu sắc được thực hiện bởi những người có động cơ thương mại.
Edmund: Đó là lý do tại sao tôi tin rằng chuyên ngành cần nhấn mạnh rằng số hóa không bao giờ chỉ là kỹ thuật. Mỗi bản quét là một hành động biên tập. Mỗi lược đồ siêu dữ liệu là một lập luận về điều gì quan trọng. Và đối tượng vật lý — dù ở bất kỳ tình trạng nào, dù ở bất kỳ địa vị nào là bản sao hay bản gốc — là thứ duy nhất chống lại việc bị hoàn toàn hấp thụ vào khung của người khác.
Vivienne: Tôi thấy mình đồng ý nhiều hơn tôi mong đợi. Có lẽ điều mà sự chuyển đổi kỹ thuật số của kho lưu trữ đòi hỏi không phải là bảo vệ vật lý chống lại kỹ thuật số, mà là một chính trị lưu trữ quyết đoán hơn nhiều — một tuyên bố rằng những quyết định này thuộc về chuyên ngành, và công chúng mà chuyên ngành phục vụ, không phải cho bất kỳ ai tình cờ nắm giữ hợp đồng số hóa.

questions.prompt: What does Edmund identify as the first and unambiguous benefit of the digitisation programme?
options: The financial savings on physical storage costs.
The improved condition of documents through reduced handling.
Access for researchers who could never visit the archive in person.
The creation of permanent backup copies immune to physical deterioration.
questions.explanation: Edmund explicitly says digitisation has made the collection accessible to researchers in Vietnam, Argentina, and rural Scotland — 'people who could never have come here in person. That is an unambiguous good.'
questions.hint: Edmund's opening concession names a specific beneficiary group.

questions.prompt: What does Edmund mean by 'the sensory dimension' of working with physical documents?
options: The aesthetic pleasure of handling beautifully crafted manuscripts.
The physical evidence — foxing, ink quality, pressure variations — that communicates information about a document's history and the emotional state of its creator.
The use of specialised equipment to analyse documents in controlled conditions.
The training required to handle fragile materials without causing damage.
questions.explanation: Edmund lists three specific sensory sources of information: foxing (storage conditions), ink quality (materials available), and pressure variations in handwriting (emotional state of writer).
questions.hint: Edmund gives three concrete examples of what the sensory dimension provides.

questions.prompt: Vivienne's counter-argument about multispectral imaging is that:
options: It is too expensive for most archives to afford.
It only works on documents that have already been physically damaged.
Digital technology is advancing rapidly enough to eventually capture everything the physical document contains.
Multispectral imaging is already superior to physical examination in most respects.
questions.explanation: Vivienne argues that Edmund's concern about current technology's limitations is 'a problem of current technology rather than digitisation in principle' — the technology is 'converging on the physical object in ways that were unimaginable twenty years ago'.
questions.hint: Vivienne's argument is about potential, not current capability.

questions.prompt: What is Edmund's rebuttal to Vivienne's point about improving technology?
options: That technology companies are unlikely to invest in archives.
That multispectral imaging captures only what was already known to look for — the problem is that future researchers may need to examine aspects no current protocol anticipates.
That the costs of advanced imaging technology exceed the archival budget.
That physical examination will always be faster and more efficient than digital alternatives.
questions.explanation: Edmund says 'Multispectral imaging captures what we already know to look for' — the deeper problem is that future researchers may ask questions about aspects of documents that current digitisation decisions never thought to record.
questions.hint: Edmund's argument is about the unknowability of future research questions.

questions.prompt: What does Edmund identify as the specific 'crisis' — distinct from digitisation itself?
options: The inadequate training of staff conducting digitisation.
The loss of funding for physical conservation alongside digitisation.
Deaccessioning — the logic that having a digital copy means the physical object is no longer needed.
The monopolisation of digitisation by a small number of vendors.
questions.explanation: Edmund explicitly says: 'The crisis — and I think it is a crisis — comes when digitisation is accompanied by deaccessioning: when the logic of \"we have a digital copy, we no longer need the physical object\" begins to take hold.'
questions.hint: Edmund makes a specific distinction between digitisation itself and what accompanies it.

questions.prompt: Vivienne challenges Edmund's concept of 'the authority of the original'. What is her challenge?
options: That the concept of originality is a Romantic myth with no practical application.
That many documents in the archive are themselves copies — scribal copies, transcriptions — making the distinction between 'original' and 'copy' problematic.
That digital records now have equal legal authority to physical ones.
That the physical condition of most documents is too poor for them to serve as reliable originals.
questions.explanation: Vivienne points out that 'many of the documents in our collection are themselves copies — scribal copies, official transcriptions' — so the assumption that the physical document is 'the original' is already theoretically complicated.
questions.hint: Vivienne questions whether the 'original' category is as stable as Edmund assumes.

questions.prompt: How does Edmund respond to Vivienne's point about copies and originals?
options: He concedes the point entirely and abandons his position.
He argues that the distinction between copy and original can be resolved through careful palaeographic analysis.
He acknowledges the complication but argues it makes preservation more important, not less — the accumulation of mediating layers increases the value of what remains.
He argues that scribal copies are more reliable than the originals they replaced.
questions.explanation: Edmund says 'I would not deny that. But the accumulation of mediating layers does not reduce the value of what remains — it makes it more important to preserve what remains, not less.'
questions.hint: Edmund's response is a concession followed by a 'but' that reverses the implication.

questions.prompt: What does Vivienne identify as the most troubling aspect of current digitisation?
options: The technical limitations of current scanning equipment.
The lack of agreed standards between different archives.
The institutional context — particularly commercial funding that gives technology companies influence over access infrastructure, metadata standards, and licensing.
The pace of digitisation, which she believes is too slow to save deteriorating materials.
questions.explanation: Vivienne says the problem is 'less the technology than the institutional context' — specifically that commercial funding gives companies data about researcher behaviour, licensing rights, and influence over metadata standards.
questions.hint: Vivienne shifts from the technology question to the question of who controls it.

questions.prompt: What does Edmund mean when he says the relationship between technology companies and scholarship is 'instrumental at best'?
options: That technology companies have developed useful tools for archive management.
That companies view scholarship purely as a means to an end — a source of data, profit, or influence — rather than having genuine scholarly values.
That the companies are using archives as marketing instruments to attract academic clients.
That technology is a neutral instrument that carries no values of its own.
questions.explanation: 'Instrumental' means treating something as a means rather than an end in itself — companies use scholarship to gain data and revenue, not because they share the values of open access and preservation.
questions.hint: Consider the philosophical distinction between intrinsic and instrumental value.

questions.prompt: Vivienne's final contribution shifts the debate's conclusion. What does she suggest the archive profession should demand?
options: A complete halt to commercially funded digitisation until new laws are passed.
Not a defence of the physical against the digital, but an assertive archival politics — that digitisation decisions belong to the profession and the public, not commercial contractors.
That all digitisation metadata be released under open-source licences.
That national governments take over all digitisation programmes from private entities.
questions.explanation: Vivienne concludes with a call for 'a much more assertive archival politics — a claim that these decisions belong to the profession, and to the public the profession serves, not to whoever happens to hold the digitisation contract'.
questions.hint: Vivienne reframes the problem from technology to professional and political agency.

questions.prompt: Edmund says 'every scan is an editorial act'. What does he mean?
options: That scanning should only be done by staff with editorial training.
That every digitisation decision — resolution, colour, what features to capture — is an interpretive choice about what matters, not a neutral technical operation.
That digital archives should be edited before publication to remove private information.
That scanning requires the same level of curatorial judgment as selecting items for exhibition.
questions.explanation: An 'editorial act' involves deciding what to include, emphasise, or exclude. Edmund argues that digitisation involves exactly such decisions — about what to capture and what to discard — making it an exercise of interpretive power, not a neutral technical process.
questions.hint: Consider what editors do and how that applies to digitisation decisions.

questions.prompt: How does Vivienne describe the way commercial digitisation projects present themselves?
options: As charitable projects serving the public good.
As technologically advanced services superior to in-house archival work.
As neutral provision of access, when they are actually a series of consequential interpretive decisions made by people with commercial incentives.
As temporary partnerships that will hand all materials back to the archive on completion.
questions.explanation: Vivienne says the project 'appears as neutral provision of access, when it is in fact a deeply consequential series of interpretive decisions made by people with commercial incentives'.
questions.hint: Vivienne contrasts how the project appears with what it actually is.

questions.prompt: What does Edmund see as the unique value of the physical object as opposed to any surrogate?
options: That it is legally binding as evidence in ways that digital copies are not.
That it resists being entirely subsumed into someone else's framework — it retains an irreducible material existence that cannot be fully captured in any set of prior decisions.
That physical objects are more emotionally resonant for researchers.
That physical storage is cheaper and more reliable than digital storage over long periods.
questions.explanation: Edmund argues that the physical object 'is the one thing that resists being entirely subsumed into someone else's framework' — its material existence is always available for fresh examination beyond what any digitisation protocol anticipated.
questions.hint: Edmund's argument is about the inescapability and openness of the physical.

questions.prompt: What is the significance of Edmund saying the retreat of physical records happens 'one administrative decision at a time'?
options: That administrative inefficiency is slowing down digitisation progress.
That the change is gradual and individually justifiable, but cumulatively constitutes a systemic transformation that is hard to identify or resist.
That archivists lack the authority to resist deaccessioning decisions made by administrators.
That better documentation of administrative decisions would prevent inappropriate deaccessioning.
questions.explanation: The phrase suggests that no single decision appears dramatic or unjustifiable, but the cumulative effect of many small decisions is the systematic withdrawal of physical records from practical access — a structural change achieved through incremental drift.
questions.hint: Think about how large changes can happen through many individually unremarkable steps.

questions.prompt: Which speaker's position shifts more significantly during the conversation?
options: Edmund — he begins sceptical of digitisation and ends accepting it entirely.
Vivienne — she begins as a straightforward proponent of digitisation and ends advocating for a politicised archival response to commercial control.
Neither — both maintain their initial positions throughout.
Both equally — they converge on a compromise position midway through.
questions.explanation: Vivienne begins defending digitisation technology and ends calling for an 'assertive archival politics' — a position that largely endorses Edmund's concerns, reframed in political rather than sensory terms. Edmund's position is consistent throughout.
questions.hint: Compare Vivienne's first turn with her final turn.

cloze.template:
Edmund: I am not a {{b1}} about this. The digitisation programme has made our collection accessible to researchers in Vietnam, in Argentina, in rural Scotland — people who could never have come here in person.
Edmund: When you work with a document over many years, you develop a relationship with it that is partly sensory. The {{b2}} on a page tells you about the storage conditions it has survived. The {{b3}} in a handwritten letter tell you about the emotional state of the person who wrote it.
Vivienne: Isn't that a problem of current technology rather than digitisation in principle? We already have {{b4}} that reveals text invisible to the naked eye.
Edmund: The problem with digital {{b5}} is that they are the product of decisions made in advance: decisions about resolution, about colour calibration, about what counts as a meaningful feature. A researcher fifty years from now may need to examine an aspect of the original that our {{b6}} never thought to record.
Edmund: The crisis comes when digitisation is accompanied by {{b7}}: when the logic of 'we have a digital copy, we no longer need the physical object' begins to take hold. The physical record retreats from practical accessibility one {{b8}} at a time.
Vivienne: There is an assumption in archival practice that the physical document is authoritative. But many of our documents are themselves copies — {{b9}}, official transcriptions. At what point does a copy become the record?
Edmund: The accumulation of {{b10}} does not reduce the value of what remains — it makes it more important to preserve what remains, not less.
Vivienne: Digitisation is being funded by partnerships with commercial entities whose interests are not identical to those of public access. When a technology company funds the digitisation of a national archive, they shape the {{b11}} that determine how documents are findable.
Edmund: The infrastructure of access is being built by actors whose relationship to scholarship is {{b12}} at best.
Vivienne: The digitisation project appears as neutral provision of access, when it is in fact a deeply {{b13}} series of interpretive decisions made by people with commercial incentives.
Edmund: Every scan is an {{b14}} act. Every metadata schema is an argument about what matters. The physical object is the one thing that resists being entirely {{b15}} into someone else's framework.
Vivienne: What the digital transformation of the archive demands is a much more assertive archival politics — a claim that these decisions belong to the profession, not to whoever happens to hold the digitisation contract.

options: technophobe
sceptic
Luddite
conservative
blanks.explanation: 'Luddite' is Edmund's specific word — a person opposed to new technology, derived from nineteenth-century factory workers who destroyed machinery.

options: watermarks
annotations
foxing
binding
blanks.explanation: 'Foxing' — brown spots from damp and mould — is the specific sensory evidence Edmund mentions that reveals storage history.

options: ink variations
margin notes
pressure variations
spelling patterns
blanks.explanation: 'Pressure variations' in handwriting — how hard the pen pressed — is Edmund's example of evidence about the writer's emotional state.

options: infrared photography
electron microscopy
multispectral imaging
ultraviolet scanning
blanks.explanation: 'Multispectral imaging' is the specific technology Vivienne cites as evidence that digital methods are converging on physical capability.

options: copies
files
surrogates
records
blanks.explanation: 'Surrogates' — substitutes for the original — is the specific archival term for digital copies that stand in for physical documents.

options: metadata systems
scanning equipment
digitisation protocols
classification standards
blanks.explanation: 'Digitisation protocols' — the established rules and standards governing the scanning process — are what Edmund says will fail to anticipate future research needs.

options: reclassification
outsourcing
deaccessioning
cataloguing
blanks.explanation: 'Deaccessioning' — formally removing an item from the collection — is the specific practice Edmund identifies as the real crisis accompanying digitisation.

options: policy decision
acquisition review
administrative decision
management review
blanks.explanation: 'Administrative decision' is the specific phrase — suggesting each step is individually innocuous but cumulatively transformative.

options: handwritten notes
official duplicates
scribal copies
printed editions
blanks.explanation: 'Scribal copies' — made by professional copyists in pre-print eras — are the specific type Vivienne uses to challenge the concept of an 'original'.

options: technical decisions
copying errors
mediating layers
interpretive frameworks
blanks.explanation: 'Mediating layers' — each stage of translation, transcription, printing, or scanning that stands between the original and the current form — is Edmund's specific concept.

options: cataloguing rules
licensing terms
metadata standards
access policies
blanks.explanation: 'Metadata standards' — the rules determining how documents are described and therefore findable — is the specific mechanism Vivienne identifies as being shaped by commercial interests.

options: exploitative
commercial
instrumental
competitive
blanks.explanation: 'Instrumental' — treating scholarship as a means to an end — is Edmund's precise characterisation of the commercial relationship to academic values.

options: political
complex
consequential
controversial
blanks.explanation: 'Deeply consequential' — having far-reaching effects — is Vivienne's word for the series of interpretive decisions embedded in digitisation.

options: interpretive
political
editorial
institutional
blanks.explanation: 'Editorial act' — a decision about what to include, emphasise, or exclude — is Edmund's term, positioning scanning as a form of authorial/editorial power.

options: absorbed
incorporated
subsumed
integrated
blanks.explanation: 'Subsumed' — absorbed into something larger in a way that loses distinctiveness — is Edmund's specific word for what commercial frameworks do to archives.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Edmund argues that every digitisation decision is an 'editorial act' that embeds assumptions about what matters. But this critique applies with equal force to the creation and organisation of physical archives — every acquisition decision, every cataloguing choice, every storage priority reflects the values and blindspots of its time. If all archival work is 'editorial' in this sense, does Edmund's argument against commercial digitisation rest on a distinction that cannot be sustained — or is there something genuinely different about the commercial context that makes the same 'editorialising' more dangerous?

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: I want to be clear that I am not a Luddite about this.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The digitisation programme has made our collection accessible to researchers in Vietnam, in Argentina, in rural Scotland — people who could never have come here in person.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: That is an unambiguous good, and I would feel dishonest if I didn't acknowledge it first.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: And yet you have reservations.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: I have reservations that I find myself unable to adequately articulate, which is perhaps the most disturbing aspect of the problem.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: When you work with a document over many years, you develop a relationship with it that is partly sensory.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The foxing on a page tells you something about the storage conditions it has survived.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The quality of the ink tells you about the materials available to the writer.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The pressure variations in a handwritten letter tell you about the emotional state of the person who wrote it.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: None of that is captured in a digital scan.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: I take your point about the sensory dimension, but isn't that a problem of current technology rather than digitisation in principle?

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: We already have multispectral imaging that reveals text invisible to the naked eye.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Three-dimensional scanning can capture paper texture and seal impressions.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: The technology is converging on the physical object in ways that were unimaginable twenty years ago.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Multispectral imaging captures what we already know to look for.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The problem with digital surrogates is that they are, by definition, the product of decisions made in advance: decisions about resolution, about colour calibration, about what counts as a meaningful feature worth capturing.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The physical document does not make those decisions.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: It simply is.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: And a researcher fifty years from now, asking a question we cannot currently anticipate, may need to examine an aspect of the original that our digitisation protocols never thought to record.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: That argument could justify never digitising anything.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Every technology involves decisions about what to capture and what to discard.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Photography made similar choices.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: A written transcript makes choices.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Even a catalogue entry embeds assumptions about what is significant.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: I grant that entirely.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The difference is that when you photograph an object or write a catalogue entry, the original remains.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The original can be consulted again.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The crisis — and I think it is a crisis, though a slow-moving one — comes when digitisation is accompanied by deaccessioning: when the logic of 'we have a digital copy, we no longer need the physical object' begins to take hold.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: That is happening?

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: In some institutions, yes.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Not dramatically, not often announced, but through a gradual shift in acquisition policy, in storage decisions, in what gets transferred to off-site facilities that in practice means items are never consulted again.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The physical record retreats from practical accessibility one administrative decision at a time.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: I want to challenge something else you said earlier — about the authority of the original.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: There is an assumption in archival practice that the physical document is the authoritative object, and that the digital surrogate is secondary.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: But that assumes we can trust our assessment of what constitutes the original.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Many of the documents in our collection are themselves copies — scribal copies, official transcriptions, copies made when the prior version was deteriorating.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: At what point does a copy become the record?

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: That is a genuine and important complication.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: And it applies with particular force to documents that have already been through multiple mediating processes: translated, transcribed, printed, microfilmed, and now scanned.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: At each stage, something is interpreted and something is lost.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: I would not deny that.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: But the accumulation of mediating layers does not reduce the value of what remains — it makes it more important to preserve what remains, not less.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: I think what I find most troubling about the current moment is less the technology than the institutional context in which it is being deployed.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Digitisation is expensive, and it is being funded, in many cases, by partnerships with commercial entities whose interests are not identical to those of public access and long-term preservation.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: When a technology company funds the digitisation of a national archive, they acquire data about how researchers interact with documents, they may hold licences that complicate future re-digitisation, and they shape the metadata standards that determine how documents are findable.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Now that is something I have been trying to put into words for years.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: The authority of the original has always been contested, but it was contested on scholarly grounds, within the community of those who cared about documents.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: What is new is that the infrastructure of access is being built by actors whose relationship to scholarship is instrumental at best.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: And who are systematically removing from the archive the traces of their own involvement.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: The digitisation project becomes invisible — it appears as neutral provision of access, when it is in fact a deeply consequential series of interpretive decisions made by people with commercial incentives.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Which is why I believe the profession needs to insist that digitisation is never merely technical.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Every scan is an editorial act.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: Every metadata schema is an argument about what matters.

sentences.speaker: Edmund
sentences.text: And the physical object — whatever its condition, whatever its status as copy or original — is the one thing that resists being entirely subsumed into someone else's framework.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: I find myself agreeing more than I expected to.

sentences.speaker: Vivienne
sentences.text: Perhaps what the digital transformation of the archive demands is not a defence of the physical against the digital, but a much more assertive archival politics — a claim that these decisions belong to the profession, and to the public the profession serves, not to whoever happens to hold the digitisation contract.
